full_text:
Antibody Validations SOP

Index:

Part 1: Biology of the target

Part 2: Criteria for antibody selection

Part 3: Sample selection

Part 4: Organizing the validation experiment

Part 5: Reporting system

Part 1: Biology of the target

Subcellular localization of expression:

A thorough understanding of the biology of the target antigen is essential. The antibody should exhibit both high sensitivity and specificity for the target antigen, characterized by strong affinity and minimal cross-reactivity. A comprehensive understanding of the expected biological localization of the signal for the target is essential. This includes several parameters:

Tissue type: Identifying the specific tissue where the target is naturally expressed.

Histologic subtype: Differentiating between tumor tissues and stroma.

Subcellular localization: Determining whether the antigen is cytoplasmic, nuclear, membrane or else.

Staining pattern: Recognizing the characteristic staining morphology, to ensure that the antibody is performing as expected.

Part 2: Criteria for antibody selection

Application type

a. Antibodies reported to perform well in immunofluorescence (IF) on manufacturer websites often fail when applied to formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) sections due to differences in sample fixation.

b. Similarly, antibodies that work in flow cytometry often do not work in CyCIF.

c. References for immunohistochemistry (IHC) for primary unconjugated antibodies performance should be prioritized because they have higher chances of working in CycIF.

Monoclonal antibodies are preferred over polyclonal:

a. Polyclonal antibodies are produced by several B-cell clones, representing a pool of different antibody clones, each binding to a distinct epitope on the target antigen. They represent a batch-specific mix that cannot be exactly reproduced once exhausted.

b. Monoclonal antibodies, derived by identical B-cell clones from a single parent cell, bind to a single, definable epitope, providing high specificity and consistency across experiments, therefore are preferred.

For rare or difficult targets, primary unconjugated antibodies are recommended, as they allow for signal amplification and provide flexibility in experimental design.

Prioritize antibodies that have been reported to work with proven efficacy from reliable sources (e.g., lab members, collaborators). Additionally, research antibodies recommended for CyCIF from the Harvard Tissue Atlas (HTA) (https://www.cycif.org/antibodies/recommended).

Review reference papers from manufacturers thoroughly, as they may provide valuable insights into protocol recommendations, antibody dilutions, secondary antibodies used, and the tissue types tested. Pre-analytic variables as variable time to fixation, inadequate fixation period, differences in fixative used, and tissue processing can all affect tissue antigenicity.

Product specifications should align with the experimental panel (regarding the requirements for panel construction, such as conjugated or unconjugated form, fluorophore, secondary antibody, and host species).

Prefer to use the same clone (and lot, if possible) that has worked previously. If using the same antibody (same clone and company) in a conjugated form, note that different fluorophores may perform differently and require independent validation. Different lots may also exhibit variability in antibody concentration, necessitating re-validation (batch-to-batch variability).

Verify whether the manufacturer provides documentation on the validation procedure for the antibody (for example applications tested, multiple cell lines, knockout cells, and siRNA-treated cells etc.).

Part 3: Sample selection

Utilize positive and negative control tissues when possible:

a. Select between PDS, IDS samples, and tissue types such as ovarian or spleen, based on the validated target.

b. Choose samples where a positive signal is anticipated. The target could be heterogeneously expressed in the target tissue. Whole sections provide more antigen-positive cells and negative internal control cells within each section, therefore it’s advisable to scan the whole section (if possible and time-efficient).

Use multiple sections of the target tissue:

Select different sections, preferably adjacent to those in your final experimental cohort. Pre-experimental variables may result in inconsistent antigen expression or staining.  Testing multiple sections helps ensure antibody reproducibility.

Use successive sections from the same block:

This allows for the comparison of different antibody dilutions within the same region between the successive sections, enabling more precise correlation of signal intensity to determine the optimal signal-to-noise ratio (Using the Avivator platform enables direct comparisons of intensity values, and assessment of the overall intensity range).

Part 4: Organizing the validation experiment

The target antibody should first be validated individually.

Test a range of dilutions, typically from 1:50 to 1:500 (range could vary depending on the antibody). Higher concentrations can lead to nonspecific staining and do not necessarily imply greater specificity.

Evaluate whether the antibody works alongside the other antibodies within the cycle in which it will be applied, to check potential cross-reactivity. Cross-reactivity between antigens occurs when an antibody raised against one specific antigen has a competing high affinity toward a different antigen. This is often the case when two antigens have similar structural regions (homology of the antibody immunogen) that the antibody recognizes.

Verify that the antibody works within the final experimental panel, including validating the order of the total cycles. Antibodies that perform well in earlier cycles may exhibit diminished efficacy in later cycles due to potential epitope exhaustion. Additionaly, there is a subset of antibodies that increase in intensity with cycle number and a similar fraction of antibodies that decrease in intensity.

For rigorous antibody evaluation, implement independent antibody strategies:

Correlate two or more antibodies that target non-overlapping epitopes of the antigen of interest. In detail, compare two or more antibodies that have the same target but with a different clone and fluorophore tested in the same section and stained at the same time. When multiple antibodies bind to different, nonoverlapping epitopes of the target and show a correlated signal, it offers clear evidence that these antibodies are specific to the target antigen.

Correlate with markers specific to the cell type expressing the antigen of interest (e.g., combine within the same cycle general immune cell markers if the antibody is located in immune cells).

Correlate the target antibody with markers that might interact (such as an interaction observed for receptors markers).

Part 5: Reporting System

Finalize the experimental results and consult your supervisor to confirm the outcome.

Report the validation results carefully:

The report should be understandable to all lab members.

Could be valuable to include the validation strategy that was followed as a small description.

Systematic criteria when reporting could include the above information:

Tissue types tested

Final dilution should be registered in the antibody database

Grading of staining intensity (e.g., weak, moderate, strong)

Exposure time applied

Staining morphology

Protocol modifications (e.g., spinning time, secondary antibody dilution)

Any limitations encountered (e.g., antibody aggregates, weak signal, high background noise, artifacts, ectopic signal that do not correlate with the expected localisation of the target antigen, bright signal derived from RBCs or blood vessels , irregular performance between samples etc.)

Cross reactivity, if observed, within the cycle or final panel

Report any findings that may not align with existing literature but demonstrate a genuine signal in your experiments

Report if an antibody should be re-evaluated

Data storage location (final stiched image)

The validation document could be within the final experimental document and registered in the spatial experimental form. Any new experiment should be registered in the experimental form.

Its advisable to stitch (utilising Ashlar) the rcpnl files from all the cycles that where tested and keep the final image for future refence and evaluation.

Suggestion: A separate hard drive dedicated to validation performance, sharing different validation strategies and outcomes from various experimenters, could be helpful in providing valuable insights into the antibodies tested and the detailed processes that were followed.

References:

-MacNeil T, Vathiotis IA, Martinez-Morilla S, Yaghoobi V, Zugazagoitia J, Liu Y, Rimm DL. Antibody validation for protein expression on tissue slides: a protocol for immunohistochemistry. Biotechniques. 2020 Dec;69(6):460-468. doi: 10.2144/btn-2020-0095. Epub 2020 Aug 27. PMID: 32852223; PMCID: PMC7807291.

-Du Z, Lin JR, Rashid R, Maliga Z, Wang S, Aster JC, Izar B, Sorger PK, Santagata S. Qualifying antibodies for image-based immune profiling and multiplexed tissue imaging. Nat Protoc. 2019 Oct;14(10):2900-2930. doi: 10.1038/s41596-019-0206-y. Epub 2019 Sep 18. PMID: 31534232; PMCID: PMC6959005.

-Bordeaux J, Welsh A, Agarwal S, Killiam E, Baquero M, Hanna J, Anagnostou V, Rimm D. Antibody validation. Biotechniques. 2010 Mar;48(3):197-209. doi: 10.2144/000113382. Erratum in: Biotechniques. 2010 May;48(5):351. PMID: 20359301; PMCID: PMC3891910.

-https://www.cycif.org/antibodies/recommended
